name: Morgan DeBaun
title: Morgan DeBaun Built a Media Empire Out of a Group Chat
dek: How a frustrated Silicon Valley product manager turned Black culture's internet conversation into a multi-platform media machine.
body_html:
Picture this: you're a young Black woman working inside Silicon Valley, scrolling through your group chat, and you realize the most important cultural conversations happening online have no real home. So what do you do? If you're Morgan DeBaun, you go build the home yourself.

From Group Chat to Game Changer

DeBaun cut her teeth at a major tech company, learning the gospel of product and growth from the inside. But the corporate ladder felt like the wrong climb. Around the mid-2010s, she co-founded Blavity — a portmanteau of "Black" and "gravity" — built on a deceptively simple idea: Black millennials weren't being served by legacy media, so meet them where they already were, which was online and very, very online.

The early days were classic scrappy-founder lore: building an audience post by post, betting that authentic voice would beat polished corporate gloss. It did. Blavity caught the cultural updraft and rode it.

Building a Whole Universe, Not Just a Blog

Here's where DeBaun's creativity really flexed. She didn't stop at a single site. She turned Blavity into a constellation — reportedly spinning up verticals covering tech, entertainment, travel, and news, each speaking fluently to a specific corner of the audience. The genius was treating culture as a portfolio: AfroTech for the engineers and founders, Travel Noire for the wanderers, Shadow and Act for the cinephiles.

It wasn't a website. It was a city, with neighborhoods for everyone.

And the flagship move? AfroTech grew into a tentpole conference — a real-world gathering that took the digital community and gave it a physical pulse, drawing crowds of Black technologists, recruiters, and dreamers under one roof. DeBaun understood something a lot of media founders miss: the brand world has to live offline too. Events, newsletters, social, video — every surface reinforced the same message of belonging.

The Come-Up Into Real Money

Media is famously brutal to monetize, but DeBaun ran Blavity like the product person she is — leaning on data, audience insight, and partnerships with major brands hungry to reach an audience they'd long ignored. Over the years the company is widely reported to have raised significant venture funding and scaled into one of the most recognizable Black-owned digital media houses around, reaching an audience often described in the tens of millions.

She also became a mentor-by-megaphone, sharing the founder playbook through her own podcast and writing, turning her come-up into a roadmap for the next wave. The wealth followed the influence, not the other way around.

What makes DeBaun a Millennials Creatives hall-of-famer isn't just that she spotted a gap. It's that she filled it with taste, structure, and a relentless sense that her community deserved a media experience built for them rather than aimed at them.

She looked at a conversation everyone else was sleeping on and turned it into gravity. Fitting, really — the whole point was to pull people in.
